quote: Hi, Carter. My name is Tim. I just missed a call from IIM Solar.
stewart_read: Tim misnames the company on the inbound call and Carter does not correct it. The intro beat — which exists specifically to establish legitimacy and answer 'is this a scam?' — never occurs. Carter moves directly into record-searching. On a recycled lead with a warm customer this may feel low-stakes, but the codex is explicit: legitimacy in the first 30 seconds is a required beat regardless of lead type.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have judged that correcting the name would create friction with an already-warm inbound caller. Rejected — there is no evidence Carter made a deliberate choice here; he simply did not execute the beat.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the intro beat requirement and ask him to walk through what he would have said if he had opened with a clean legitimacy anchor. The question is not whether Tim needed convincing — he didn't — but whether Carter has the habit of executing the beat at all.

stewart_read: Carter correctly states the threshold as 670 when Tim approximates it. One minute later Tim reports 669 and Carter accepts it. The codex flags a known discrepancy — SESSION18_fcbf33c1 rep stated 650, schema says 670 — and the policy question of whether the setter should DQ on stated credit or pass to the closer's pull is unresolved. Carter's acceptance of 669 may be within policy if the rule is 'let the closer's pull decide.' What is not ambiguous: Carter does not flag the one-point gap for the closer. The closer walks in blind to a potential credit issue.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have accepted 669 as close enough under a floor norm that rounds to the threshold. This is plausible but unverifiable from the transcript — and either way the handoff flag is missing.
coaching_implication: Lead with the handoff failure, not the acceptance decision. Ask Carter: 'What did you put in the notes for the closer about the credit number?' If the answer is nothing, that is the coaching gap. The acceptance question is secondary and requires Kenny to resolve the policy first.

quote: That's fine. That was great. Oh, I just went up 53 points, and I'm Okay. Pretty close to it.
stewart_read: Tim has actively monitored his credit score and improved it by 53 points specifically to qualify for this program. This is a high-commitment signal — he bought a house, tracked his credit, and called back. Carter responds with 'You're good. Yeah. Take your time.' — functional but flat. The 53-point improvement is a trust-building anchor Carter could have used to validate Tim's effort and deepen his investment in the appointment.
coaching_implication: Ask Carter what he noticed about Tim's 53-point improvement. A suggested rephrase: 'That's real work — you tracked that and got it done. That's exactly the kind of thing that makes this worth doing.' The goal is not flattery; it is anchoring Tim's prior effort to the upcoming appointment so the sit feels like a payoff, not a sales call.

quote: No. It's it that'll work. That'll be good.
stewart_read: Carter accepts 669 against his own stated threshold of 670 and immediately pivots to scheduling. The booking proceeds on a credit number that is at the edge of — or one point below — the stated policy threshold. The closer is not warned. This is a conditional booking in the sense that the credit qualification is unresolved at the setter stage and will surface again at the proposal.
alternative_hypothesis: If Ion's policy is 'let the closer's pull decide,' Carter's acceptance is correct procedure. The failure is still the missing handoff flag regardless of which policy applies.
coaching_implication: This moment is the handoff brief in miniature. Carter should have noted in the CRM: 'Customer self-reports 669 — one point below stated threshold. Closer should run the pull and be prepared for a potential credit conversation.' Without that note, the closer is set up for a surprise.

quote: Yeah. We can run through that with you. It looks like you looked into it.
stewart_read: Carter acknowledges the prior interaction and moves toward scheduling without re-running any verify items. No address confirmation, no co-owner check, no bill collection, no roof age, no utility company. The codex requires 6-of-8 verify items for a qualified appointment. Carter's implicit logic — 'they already went through it' — is stated explicitly at 02:21, but there is no evidence the prior record was located or confirmed. The verify section is skipped entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have judged that re-running verify on a recycled warm lead would feel redundant and create friction. Rejected — Carter explicitly states at 00:48 that he cannot find the record, so the prior data is not confirmed as on file.
coaching_implication: Carter needs to understand that 'they went through it before' is only a valid shortcut if the record is confirmed in the system. When the record cannot be located, the verify section is not optional. Ask Carter: 'If the closer shows up and the address is wrong or there's a co-owner who wasn't included, whose problem is that?'

quote: I won't be home until, like, 04:30 at the earliest. I need to go pick up my kids, so probably no earlier than five.
stewart_read: Tim mentions picking up his kids, which is a scheduling detail, not a co-owner signal. The missed verify gap here is that Carter never asked about co-owners or anyone else on the title at any point in the call. The codex marks title_co_owners as required and critical for spouse/co-decider coordination at button-up. Carter's failure to ask is a verify completion gap — the kids mention is incidental context, not the trigger.
coaching_implication: The coaching point is the missing verify item, not this specific line. Ask Carter to walk through the verify checklist and identify which items he covered on this call. The answer will be close to zero, which opens the broader verify discipline conversation.

quote: Alright. K. Awesome. Yeah. We're planning on fixing tomorrow, and then I mean, you went through it all before. But I guess since they already went through it, they'll just make sure your credit's good at it. Pressure and Yeah. Go through it. But it should probably be about forty minutes is also.
stewart_read: Carter's button-up is missing four of six required elements: no 'at your computer' requirement, no 'is that fair' handshake, no text follow-up promise, and no phone-appointment specification. The one-hour block is approximated as 'about forty minutes.' The codex is explicit that skipping the computer requirement raises no-show rate and that the 'is that fair' verbal handshake is predictive of show rate. Carter's close is conversational but structurally incomplete.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have judged that a warm, self-directed customer does not need a formal button-up. Rejected — the button-up elements exist to protect the sit rate, not to persuade the customer. Tim's warmth does not eliminate the no-show risk.
coaching_implication: Walk Carter through the button-up checklist against this call verbatim. Ask him to identify what he said and what he skipped. The 'is that fair' handshake and the computer requirement are the two highest-leverage missing elements — prioritize those in the drill.

quote: Alright. Appreciate it. Yeah. Of course. We'll we'll see you tomorrow.
stewart_read: The call ends with no mention of a follow-up text. The codex requires a personalized text with a tieback to something specific the customer said. Tim gave Carter a clear tieback anchor — the 53-point credit improvement and the new house purchase — neither of which appears in the close. The text follow-up is a sit-protection tool; its absence on a one-point-below-threshold credit booking is a compounding risk.
coaching_implication: Ask Carter what text he sent after this call. If none, use this as the teaching moment for why the text matters specifically on conditional bookings — it is the last touchpoint before the closer calls, and a personalized message reinforces Tim's commitment to the appointment.
